docs.title: LLM Perspective: JIT Agent UX
docs.summary: From the perspective of an orchestrating LLM, enumerates the JIT agent tool surface that would deliver pi-context-equivalent UX. Names concrete missing/broken tools across discovery (list-agents / list-dispatch-models / inspect-agent), authoring (author-agent-spec fixed / scaffold-agent-spec / validate-agent), dispatch (call-agent fixed / agent-trace), runtime (session linkage). Cites FB-001..016 as the friction items each surface kills.
key_claims: JIT agent UX should mirror pi-context UX: read-surface + author-surface + validate-surface + introspect-surface all queryable + tool-native (no session-log grep)
Authoring requires scaffold-agent-spec + validate-agent + a dispatch-model registry — the orchestrating LLM cannot author correctly against a missing model registry (FB-007)
Dispatch requires call-agent to return structured output (FB-009) + agent-trace history (FB-016) + zero-token detection (FB-015)
Each surface gap has a concrete FB-NNN friction item naming the lived failure
docs.scope: JIT agent tool-surface UX from the orchestrating-LLM perspective; what the framework should provide to an LLM-user dispatching subagents

surface_kills_mapping.claim_text: Like `read-config` for models. Returns every `provider/modelId` pair available for dispatch with context windows and capabilities. I never guess a model string. Kills FB-007.

Like `write-schema`. I pass a spec object. It validates against the canonical schema. It dry-runs compile. It checks the model exists. If it writes, the spec is dispatchable. Kills FB-001, FB-002, FB-003, FB-005, FB-006.

Returns the agent's output as structured content, not a status summary. If the agent produces JSON with an output schema, I get parsed JSON. If text, I get the text. I also get token usage, model used, duration. I can process the output without grepping session logs. Kills FB-009, FB-015.

Like reading a session log but tool-native. Returns last N dispatches for an agent with input, output, token usage, model, duration, errors. I see the history without leaving the tool surface. Kills FB-016.

docs.title: Pi-Context May 29 2026 LLM Perspective
docs.summary: Earlier perspective doc enumerating 11 customization surfaces (registries the in-pi LLM expects to drive) that pi-context exposes — read-config / write-schema / amend-config / write-block / migrations + 6 others. Cited in FGAP-136 as the source of the 11-registry in-pi customization promise. Established that the in-pi customization should extend to schema-evolution-with-data (which FGAP-136 closed via write-schema-migration tool).
key_claims: Pi-context exposes 11 customization-registry surfaces accessible to the in-pi LLM via tool dispatch
Schema-evolution with existing data requires migration-tool surface (gap subsequently closed by FGAP-136)
Customization-as-tool-driven is the canonical pattern downstream consumers should be able to rely on
docs.scope: Pi-context registry surfaces from the in-pi LLM perspective; what customization the LLM can drive via tools
